# American AI Skepticism Reaches New High: 55% Fear Harm

2026-04-01

A new national poll released by Quinnipiac University on March 30, 2026,
reveals a striking shift in American attitudes toward artificial
intelligence. **Fifty-five percent** of respondents now say AI will do
more harm than good in their day-to-day lives—an 11 percentage point
increase since April 2025.

The findings arrive at a pivotal moment for the AI industry, which has
seen unprecedented investment and deployment across sectors ranging from
healthcare to defense. Despite technological advances and aggressive
marketing from leading AI companies, public trust appears to be
faltering.

## Key Findings

The poll surveyed over 1,500 registered voters and highlighted several
concerning trends:

-   **55% believe AI will cause more harm than good** in daily life
-   Only 29% expressed confidence that AI will benefit society
-   Concerns span job displacement, privacy, and algorithmic bias
-   Skepticism cuts across political party lines

## Industry Response

AI companies have taken note. Anthropic’s recent study involving 81,000
participants sought to understand public hopes and fears surrounding AI.
Meanwhile, OpenAI and Google have increasingly emphasized safety
initiatives and “responsible AI” frameworks in their public
communications.

The White House’s National Policy Framework for AI, released March 20,
2026, attempts to address some concerns through federal preemption of
state-level AI laws and liability shields for developers. However,
critics argue the framework lacks enforceability.

## What This Means

The growing skepticism presents a challenge for AI companies seeking
broader adoption. Enterprise adoption continues to rise, but
consumer-facing AI products face mounting resistance. Recent
incidents—including Google’s Gemini facing a wrongful death lawsuit and
debates over AI’s role in the Iran military operation—have fueled
negative sentiment.

For policymakers, the poll underscores the urgency of crafting clear
regulations that balance innovation with public protection. With
state-level AI laws emerging across the country, a coherent national
approach remains elusive.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Source: [Quinnipiac University Poll](https://quinnipiac.edu), [LA
Times](https://latimes.com)*
